lens.included_activities: U.S. lower-middle-market contract dryers, freeze-dryers, dehydrators, ingredient processors, blenders, and private-label producers supplying recurring drying, milling, blending, filling, packaging, labeling, and quality-assurance services for fruits, vegetables, soup mixes, bouillon, and related human-food ingredients.
lens.excluded_activities: Own-brand-only manufacturers, captive plants, brokers and distributors without production responsibility, pet-food-only operations, non-control financing vehicles, and processors whose primary output is classified outside NAICS 311423.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Food and beverage manufacturers, consumer brands, retailers, foodservice suppliers, and institutional customers purchase repeated batches or capacity under tolling, co-manufacturing, private-label, ingredient-supply, and packaging arrangements, priced by pound, batch, unit, or committed capacity.

a.bridge: BLS measures occupation employment and wages for broader NAICS 311400, not task-level AI exposure for 311423 contract processors. The estimate maps exposure to production planning, raw-material and energy optimization, quality records, moisture and process-data review, procurement, customer documentation, forecasting, maintenance analysis, and inspection support, with low exposure assigned to manual handling, sanitation, maintenance execution, and equipment tending. Four-digit aggregation and the absence of plant task data can bias either direction.
a.rationale: The broader preserving sector is dominated by production (50.95% of employment), material moving (17.08%), and maintenance (9.18%), limiting pure software substitution. Dried-food operators nevertheless generate process, recipe, quality, inventory, and customer-specification data that can support AI-assisted decisions and avoid administrative or technical hiring.
a.change_evidence: Conduct payroll-weighted task and time studies at 10-15 independent air-drying, freeze-drying, and dry-blending co-manufacturers, explicitly identifying already automated controls and remaining work by role.
caveats: OEWS is for NAICS 311400 and employers of all sizes, not 311423 LMM firms.
Conventional dryers, conveyors, packaging lines, and PLC automation are not counted unless AI changes current labor tasks.

rho.bridge: Deloitte reports smart-manufacturing adoption and outcomes across industries and sizes rather than five-year implementation of the specific AI-exposed task share in LMM dried-food plants. The estimate uses reported productivity and automation priorities as a feasibility anchor and discounts for integration, sensor coverage, cybersecurity, validation, food-safety controls, scarce technical staff, and smaller capital budgets. Broader smart-manufacturing outcomes likely bias AI implementation upward.
rho.rationale: Deloitte respondents reported 7%-20% employee-productivity improvement and 10%-15% unlocked capacity after smart-manufacturing implementation, with 46% placing process automation among their top two investment priorities. Dried processes can be data-rich and repeatable, but moisture control, product variability, contamination risks, recipe accuracy, and customer validation require staged deployment and human review.
rho.change_evidence: Obtain deployment, exception, payback, and labor-hour results from MES/ERP copilots, dryer optimization, machine vision, predictive maintenance, recipe management, QA-documentation, and order-planning projects at LMM contract dryers, including failures.
caveats: The implementation survey includes technologies other than AI and likely overrepresents larger firms.
Food-safety and moisture-control obligations slow substitution but do not prevent decision support.

e.rationale: The NAICS category includes own-brand and merchant ingredient manufacturers that do not fit the outsourced-service lens, but toll drying, custom ingredient processing, co-manufacturing, and private-label packaging are established models. Capital-intensive drying capacity and specialized process know-how make customer outsourcing plausible across both ingredients and finished products.
e.change_evidence: Classify all 69 dataset-estimated LMM firms through websites, certifications, customer lists, plant capabilities, and management calls; verify the share of revenue from recurring external tolling, co-manufacturing, or private-label contracts.
caveats: Company examples demonstrate that the model exists but do not measure prevalence.
The provided LMM firm count is an EBITDA-band estimate and may include firms with mixed product classifications.

s5.bridge: Gallup measures five-year sale or transfer intentions among U.S. employer-business owners across industries, not completed qualifying control transfers among 311423 firms. The 22% employer rate anchors the estimate; a range allows for failed plans, unplanned transfers, capital intensity, specialized buyers, and food-processing acquisition activity. Intentions likely overstate completions, while confidential and unplanned deals offset part of that bias.
s5.rationale: Gallup found 22% of employer-business owners planned to sell or transfer within five years. Food-processing transactions rose from 20 in 2023 to 52 in 2024, supporting buyer activity but not a denominator-based rate for dried-food co-manufacturers.
s5.change_evidence: Build a five-year ownership-transition cohort for eligible firms using PitchBook, Capital IQ, Axial, state records, USDA/FDA establishment histories, lenders, and food-industry brokers, excluding minority financings, closures, and internal reorganizations.
caveats: Stated intentions are not completed transactions.
Published deal data undercount small private transfers and overrepresent larger transactions.

q.rationale: Specialized capacity, product-development know-how, validated processes, short runs, and customer qualification can make contract drying less easily rebid than commodity packing, allowing operators to retain yield, uptime, scheduling, and error-reduction gains. Retention falls where tolling is transparent, customers audit costs, contracts reprice annually, or competing capacity is available.
q.change_evidence: Review a representative contract set for tolling formulas, energy and raw-material pass-through, productivity-sharing clauses, capacity reservations, qualification costs, term, audit rights, customer concentration, and realized renewal margins after process improvements.
caveats: Bespoke freeze-drying and commodity dehydration have very different bargaining dynamics.
No public evidence directly measures retained AI benefit over five years.

d5.rationale: The service basket is diversified across industrial ingredients, soup mixes, bouillon, fruit and vegetable components, outdoor and institutional meals, private label, and newer freeze-dried formats. Shelf stability, reduced shipping weight, convenience, and product innovation support modest growth, while weak aggregate fruit consumption, fresh/frozen substitution, imported ingredients, and novelty-category volatility cap the estimate.
d5.change_evidence: Combine Circana/Nielsen unit volumes for dried foods with customer shipment tonnage, contracted capacity, industrial ingredient volumes, import/export data, and deflated 311423 shipments, segmented by air-dried, freeze-dried, dry mix, and bouillon.
caveats: Public demand evidence does not cleanly isolate 311423 or contract-manufactured output.
Growth in premium freeze-dried niches may be offset by mature dried-fruit and soup-mix categories.

o.bridge: FDA guidance establishes physical process-control and food-safety obligations for drying, packaging, moisture, recipe management, and contamination prevention, rather than measuring future operator-required quantity. The estimate translates the need for specialized physical equipment and accountable controls into a high operator-retention share. It may overstate retention if customers insource equipment or source finished ingredients from imports.
o.rationale: Drying requires physical raw-material preparation, controlled moisture removal, handling, testing, milling or blending, and moisture-resistant packaging. FDA guidance identifies freeze-drying, forced-air drying, and spray drying as U.S. dehydration controls and emphasizes water activity, recipe management, correct drying, storage, and packaging. Software can optimize these activities but cannot supply the physical transformation alone.
o.change_evidence: Interview food-brand and ingredient procurement teams about five-year insourcing, imported finished-ingredient substitution, software-only alternatives, equipment ownership, qualification requirements, and responsibility for process and food safety.
caveats: FDA obligations support accountable physical operations, not continued outsourcing to the same operator.
Imported finished ingredients can displace U.S. operators without eliminating end demand.

narrative.executive_view: The relevant segment consists of independent toll dryers, freeze-dryers, custom ingredient processors, and private-label producers that repeatedly transform customer materials or specifications. AI can improve scheduling, process analysis, quality records, maintenance, forecasting, and customer workflows, but the bulk of labor and value creation remains physical. Diverse end markets and shelf-stability benefits support modest demand growth, with imported finished ingredients and customer insourcing as important threats.
narrative.ai_impact: AI can assist dryer loading and cycle analysis, energy and yield optimization, raw-material planning, recipe checks, moisture and quality-data review, maintenance diagnosis, inspection triage, specification comparison, traceability records, and order service. Physical preparation, loading, drying, sanitation, maintenance execution, milling, blending, packaging, and exception response remain operator tasks.
narrative.value_capture: Retention is strongest where capacity is scarce, customer qualification is costly, runs are bespoke, and gains improve yield, uptime, quality, or delivery. Commodity tolling, transparent cost formulas, annual rebids, and concentrated customers expose labor savings to pass-through. Contract detail matters more than list pricing.
narrative.firm_availability: Contract drying and co-manufacturing are visible operating models, but the code also includes branded and merchant-product firms outside the lens. Cross-industry owner intentions and food-processing deal activity make transfers plausible; the actual eligible target set still requires firm-by-firm verification.
narrative.demand_durability: The basket spans mature dried fruit and soup mixes as well as industrial ingredients, private label, institutional meals, and expanding freeze-dried applications. Shelf life, lower weight, convenience, and specialized processing support demand, while fresh alternatives, imports, and category fashion create a wide range. Surviving demand remains highly operator-dependent because dehydration is a physical controlled process.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: Key risks include raw-material seasonality, energy intensity, moisture excursions, Salmonella and allergen contamination, rehydration during storage, customer concentration, equipment downtime, imported ingredients, and uncertain novelty demand. The evidence does not directly measure task exposure, eligible-firm prevalence, or category-weighted unit growth.
narrative.principal_driver: Specialized drying capacity and process qualification make recurring customer production physically necessary and relatively sticky.
narrative.principal_weakness: The code mixes commodity ingredients with premium niches, leaving eligibility, pricing power, and demand growth poorly measured.

sources.title: North American Industry Classification System: 311423 Dried and Dehydrated Food Manufacturing
sources.supports: Defines 311423 as drying or dehydrating fruits, vegetables, soup mixes and bouillon, and drying ingredients packaged with other purchased ingredients.

sources.title: May 2023 National Industry-Specific Occupational Employment and Wage Estimates: NAICS 311400
sources.supports: Reports the broader sector's employment and wage mix, including 50.95% production, 17.08% transportation/material moving, 9.18% maintenance, and 5.73% office support.

sources.title: 2025 Smart Manufacturing and Operations Survey
sources.supports: Reports 7%-20% employee-productivity improvement, 10%-15% unlocked capacity, and 46% of manufacturers ranking process automation among their top two investment priorities in a 600-respondent survey.

sources.title: Draft Guidance for Industry: Hazard Analysis and Risk-Based Preventive Controls for Human Food
sources.supports: Identifies freeze-drying, forced-air drying, and spray drying as primary U.S. dehydration controls and discusses shelf stability, rehydration-resistant packaging, recipe management, moisture, storage, mycotoxins, and sorting.

sources.title: Water Activity in Foods
sources.supports: Explains water activity measurement and states that food stability depends on reaching and maintaining suitable water activity through processing and storage.

sources.title: Co-Manufacturing and Contract Freeze-Drying
sources.supports: Documents bespoke contract drying, private-label filling, sourcing, packaging, labeling, quality control, and small- or large-batch services for food-industry customers.

sources.title: Flatiron Food Factory Custom Freeze-Drying Solutions
sources.supports: Documents U.S. custom freeze-drying, bulk ingredient processing, co-packing, recipe development, milling, tolling, storage, and production from 500 to 100,000 pounds.

sources.title: Most Small-Business Owners Lack a Succession Plan
sources.supports: Reports that 22% of employer-business owners planned to sell or transfer ownership within five years, based on a fall 2024 survey of 1,264 U.S. owners.

sources.title: Food Production Mergers and Acquisitions Update
sources.supports: Reports 144 food-production transactions in 2024 and growth in food-processing transactions from 20 in 2023 to 52 in 2024.

sources.title: Peeling Open U.S. Fruit Consumption Trends
sources.supports: Reports total U.S. fruit availability declining from 0.95 cup-equivalents per person per day in 2003 to 0.82 in 2021 and identifies dried fruit within the measured forms.
